model_summary: Target seven complaint-derived criteria (acute somatic episodes, unpredictability distress, threat appraisals, low acceptance of medical reassurance, avoidance of leaving home, interoceptive hypervigilance, and functional/emotional impact) and eight modifiable predictors drawn from high-ranking psycho-behavioral levers (cognitive reappraisal, interoceptive exposure, distress-tolerance breathing, graded/behavioral exposure, sleep routine, physical activity, objective autonomic arousal, and psychoeducation engagement). Sampling emphasizes momentary EMA (5 prompts/day) for fast-changing experience and daily diaries + passive phone/wearable streams for behaviors and physiology. Design balances within-person variance, feasibility, and low redundancy to produce an estimable initial gVAR-ready dataset.
variable_selection_notes: I prioritized constructs that directly map to the complaint decomposition (threat appraisal, interoceptive attention, avoidance, somatic intensity, unpredictability distress, response to reassurance, functional impact). To minimize collinearity I selected one broad self-regulation skill (cognitive reappraisal) rather than multiple overlapping cognitive constructs, and one behavioral activation/exposure predictor instead of many activity subtypes. Predictors emphasize psycho-behavioral, low-risk interventions flagged by the HyDe and LLM mapping (exposure, interoceptive work, psychoeducation, distress tolerance, sleep hygiene) and a small set of passive signals (steps, GPS distance-from-home, HR-spike counts) to objectively capture avoidance and arousal. Uncertainty / bias notes: passive wearable signals may be missing for some participants (coverage bias); some predictor–criterion links (e.g., sleep -> threat appraisal) are weaker/indirect and scored lower. Trade-offs: fewer predictors improves gVAR identifiability but may miss niche mechanisms (e.g., neuromodulation), which can be added later if initial signals justify it.

momentary_ema.notes: Five prompts/day balances measurement density to capture short-term dynamics (0–6h/0–24h lags) while limiting burden. Blocks include jitter to reduce predictability and response batching.

daily_diary.notes: Morning diary captures last-night sleep and previous-day avoidance/impact summary (reduces redundancy with momentary EMA).

passive_streams.notes: Continuous passive capture (when available) aggregated to per-prompt windows (since last prompt) and daily summaries to align with EMA windows; includes GPS-derived max distance-from-home per day and per-prompt, daily step counts, and wearable HR-spike counts per EMA window.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Aim for N_obs >= ~100 (minimum) per person for 1st-order gVAR stability; with 140 momentary prompts + 28 diaries and passive aggregates the expected observation count per variable is sufficient for within-person gVAR. Preprocess: within-person detrending (linear/time-of-day), person-mean centering and unit scaling (z-score) for continuous/Likert measures. For bounded Likert variables, use within-person z-scoring; for skewed passive counts (steps, HR spikes) consider log1p or square-root transforms. Check stationarity visually and with augmented Dickey-Fuller per participant; include time-of-day fixed effects or periodic splines if rhythmicity is observed.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Momentary EMA items (1–9 Likert) expected to show medium–high within-person variability (C01, C02, C03, C06). Passive signals (steps, GPS) offer higher objective variance for behavioral endpoints (C05). Daily diaries provide lower-frequency signals useful for longer-lag (24h) effects (C04, C05, C07).
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Main risks: EMA nonresponse (fatigue) and wearable absence. Mitigation: brief items (single 1–9 questions), prompt reminders, optional passive-only fallback (reduce EMA frequency if adherence < 50%), imputation strategies and missingness modelling (MAR assumptions verification).

safety_notes: No acute self-harm or psychosis risk is described in the complaint. Safety guidance: include brief crisis resources in the app and an option to pause exposure tasks and contact clinician if symptoms escalate. Interoceptive exposures should be taught or recommended with screening for medical contraindications. Do not provide medical diagnostic statements or medication dosing; encourage users with new/worsening somatic signs to follow up with their clinician.

criteria_variables.label: Acute somatic symptom intensity and frequency

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how intense were any stomach cramps, nausea, heart racing, or dizziness at their worst? (Rate the worst intensity you felt)
measurement.notes: Use single worst-intensity rating per window; optionally add an event flag if an episode occurred (yes/no) for secondary analyses.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; event counts log1p if used

criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable label is Acute somatic symptom intensity and frequency with EMA self-report assessing multiple interoceptive signals (stomach cramps, nausea, heart racing, dizziness) for the worst intensity since last prompt. There is no exact leaf for multi-symptom acute interoceptive intensity. The closest operational match among candidates is somatic_symptom_burden_high, which captures elevated somatic symptom experience across domains. This leaf provides a single, parsable operational construct aligned with the intended domain (somatic symptom burden/intensity) suitable for initial gVAR-ready mapping while avoiding overfitting to a single symptom-type.

criteria_variables.label: Low acceptance of medical reassurance / perceived invalidation

measurement.item_or_signal: Thinking about yesterday and any medical feedback you've had, how much did tests or clinician reassurance reduce your concern? (1 = not at all/reassurance increased mistrust, 9 = reassurance greatly reduced concern)
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: 1-9 Likert (1=made it worse/none, 9=greatly reduced concern) — note: for model consistent polarity we will invert so higher = worse (low acceptance)
measurement.notes: Because reassurance events are relatively rare, the item measures perceived impact; considered lower-frequency but important for functional trust modelling.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: invert then within-person z-score; treat as lower-frequency variable

criteria_variables.label: Avoidance of leaving home / travel avoidance

measurement.channel: smartphone daily diary (supported by passive GPS)

measurement.notes: Supported by passive GPS-derived max_distance_from_home (daily) to provide objective validation and to separate perceived avoidance vs actual movement.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; GPS distance log1p for skew

measurement.notes: Daily capture of overall impact reduces redundancy with high-frequency momentary items.

predictor_variables.label: Cognitive reappraisal / threat-interpretation reframing (skill use)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Cognitive reappraisal reduces catastrophic threat appraisals and downstream anxiety, decreasing avoidance and functional impact.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Practice labeling thoughts and reframing 'danger' interpretations to benign explanations; short guided reappraisal exercises via app.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you use a cognitive reappraisal or reframe (e.g., reinterpreting sensations as non-dangerous)?
measurement.notes: Records both use and intensity of cognitive skill; useful for temporal coupling with threat appraisals.

expected_effects.rationale: Reappraisal is expected to reduce threat interpretations within hours.
Reducing threat appraisals reduces distress about unpredictability over short-to-medium lags.
Sustained reappraisal can reduce functional impact over longer windows.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Safe; ensure guidance avoids invalidating user experience while encouraging reinterpretation.

feasibility.notes: Simple self-report item; expected stable compliance with momentary EMA.

predictor_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / cognitive_restructuring / emotion_linked_reappraisal / threat_and_anxiety / threat_appraisal_reframe
predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best match is the leaf 'threat_appraisal_reframe' within Cognitive Reappraisal. The variable describes using cognitive reappraisal to reinterpret threat/sensations as non-dangerous, which directly maps to reframing threat appraisals. The measurement is EMA self-report of 'to what extent did you use a cognitive reappraisal or reframe', aligning with the threat_appraisal_reframe construct. This is more specific than general cognitive restructuring and directly targets threat interpretation, fitting the intended predictor as a momentary psycho-behavioral lever.

predictor_variables.label: Interoceptive exposure / somatic experiential practice
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Planned, guided interoceptive exposures (e.g., intentionally eliciting sensations in safe context) aim to recalibrate sensitivity and reduce catastrophic appraisals and hypervigilance.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Short guided exercises (e.g., light hyperventilation, spinning chair when safe) under instruction or simulated exposures like holding breath exercises — done only with appropriate guidance and contraindications screened.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you do any interoceptive exposure or body-focused practice? If yes, rate how much (1=not at all, 9=intense session).
measurement.notes: Also capture type/count in optional free-text for exploratory analyses. Safety: avoid unsupervised high-risk exposures; include screening before recommending.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; exposure counts log1p if used

expected_effects.rationale: Interoceptive exposure should reduce hypervigilance and scanning over hours to days.
Exposure may reduce reported symptom intensity or the perceived peak via learning processes.
Relearning about sensations reduces threat appraisals over days.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Interoceptive exposures should be introduced with clinician oversight when possible; avoid exposures contraindicated by medical conditions.

feasibility.notes: Self-report of practice feasible; adherence may vary.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best match: Interoceptive exposure implemented as part of exposure/inhibitory-learning protocol within CBT. The variable 'Interoceptive exposure / somatic experiential practice' corresponds to actual interoceptive exposure activities (e.g., breathing/heartbeat sensations) measured via EMA as 'did you do any interoceptive exposure' with intensity rating. Leaf 2607 precisely encodes 'interoceptive_exposure_when_indicated' under Exposure_and_Inhibitory_Learning, capturing the targeted mechanism of recalibrating sensitivity and reducing catastrophic appraisals and hypervigilance. Other leaves either describe framework, safety behaviors, or more distant concepts; this one aligns with the measurement item and the intended predictor mechanism.

predictor_variables.label: Distress-tolerance breathing / downregulation practice
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Short breathing or grounding practices rapidly downregulate sympathetic arousal and can reduce episode severity and distress.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Guided diaphragmatic breathing, paced breathing apps, 4-4-6 breathing, grounding via 5 senses.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you use a breathing or grounding technique to manage symptoms? Rate use intensity (1=not at all, 9=very much).
measurement.notes: Short, low-burden item that may show quick effects within the same prompt window.

expected_effects.rationale: Breathing can acutely reduce intensity and perceived severity in the short term.
Rapid downregulation reduces immediate distress about unpredictability.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Safe; encourage medical clearance if major cardiopulmonary disease.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility and expected within-person variance.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable label corresponds to distress-tolerance breathing within DBT Skills-Based Therapy (Third Wave/Contextual). The measurement item (EMA self-report: 'Since the last prompt, did you use a breathing or grounding technique...? Rate use intensity 1-9') directly maps to the leaf 'breathing_to_downshift_arousal' under distress_tolerance_skills. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates for a distress-tolerance breathing/downregulation practice in the DBT framework, aligning with the pseudoprofile emphasis on distress-tolerance and rapid arousal downregulation. The context supports momentary EMA capture of practice usage and intensity.

predictor_variables.label: Graded behavioral exposure (leaving-home attempts / activation)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Planned, graded attempts to leave home counter avoidance, promote inhibitory learning, and increase confidence functioning outside home.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Short stepwise outings (e.g., to end of street, short walk to nearby store) with pre-specified goals and coping plans.

measurement.channel: smartphone GPS + smartphone daily diary for intention/effort
measurement.item_or_signal: Passive: max_distance_from_home (meters) in last 24h and per-prompt window. Self-report (daily): rate degree of leaving-home attempt/effort (1-9).
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: GPS distance in meters (daily); 1-9 Likert for self-report
measurement.recall_window: past 24 hours (daily) and per-prompt windows for GPS when available
measurement.notes: GPS-derived objective behavior reduces self-report bias. Privacy: compute distance_from_home on-device or send aggregated metrics only.

expected_effects.rationale: Successful graded exposure should reduce avoidance (daily).
Behavioral activation reduces functional impact over days.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure exposures are safe and feasible; include emergency planning for severe episodes.

feasibility.notes: Passive GPS may be unavailable for some users; missingness must be handled.

predictor_variables.label: Sleep routine / last-night sleep quality
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Poor sleep increases physiological reactivity and lowers tolerance for somatic sensations; improving sleep may reduce somatic episode frequency/intensity and daily impact.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Regular bed/wake times, stimulus control, brief sleep hygiene adjustments.

measurement.item_or_signal: How well did you sleep last night? (1 = very poorly, 9 = very well). Also capture total sleep time estimate (hours).
measurement.notes: Morning diary captures sleep quality and duration for daily-lag analyses with symptoms.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score for Likert; log1p if duration skewed

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep may reduce next-day somatic sensitivity and episode intensity.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Safe; flag for referral if severe insomnia or sleep disorders suspected.

feasibility.notes: Morning diary is low-burden and reliable for sleep quality.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: The variable Sleep routine / last-night sleep quality maps to BIO Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration / Sleep_Routines_and_Practices. Among the candidate leaves, Sleep_diary (idx 4549) is the best match because it represents a daily self-assessment log (morning diary) that captures both sleep quality and total sleep time. This aligns with measurement.mode EMA_self_report and measurement.item_or_signal (last night sleep quality and hours slept). It is the most specific operational construct for a daily sleep routine measure within the Sleep_Routines_and_Practices domain, whereas Sleep_quality_rating alone would miss duration data. The pseudoprofile notes emphasize daily-lag analyses using morning diaries, which 4549 directly supports.

predictor_variables.label: Physical activity (daily steps)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Physical activity may reduce avoidance, improve mood and resilience, and reduce functional impact over days.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Daily walking goals, short structured walks as exposure/activation.

measurement.item_or_signal: Total steps in last 24 hours (phone or wearable-derived)
measurement.notes: Objective metric for activation and a correlate of leaving-home behavior.

expected_effects.rationale: More steps correlate with leaving-home and reduced avoidance.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Consider privacy; share only aggregated counts.

feasibility.notes: Phone step counts available for many but not all users; missingness expected.

predictor_variables.label: Autonomic arousal: HR-spike counts (wearable)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Objective autonomic markers (heart rate spikes) index physiological arousal during episodes and may precede subjective intensity.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Used for monitoring and biofeedback; increases self-awareness and guides timing of downregulation exercises.

measurement.channel: consumer wearable (e.g., wrist-worn HR sensor) or phone-based HR if available
measurement.item_or_signal: Count of HR spikes above baseline threshold in each 'since-last-prompt' window (and daily total); also % time above threshold
measurement.notes: Important objective correlate of C01; missing for those without wearables — treat as optional stratified analysis.

expected_effects.rationale: Higher HR spike counts are associated with reports of stronger/visible somatic episodes in the same window.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Wearable data sensitive; obtain consent and process aggregated metrics only.

feasibility.notes: Useful when available but will be missing for many; include as auxiliary variable rather than required.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable label 'Autonomic arousal: HR-spike counts (wearable)' corresponds to objective heart rate data captured via wearable devices. The most specific leaf among candidates is 'heart_rate_monitor' under wearables_and_home_monitoring, which directly represents HR measurement from wearable sensors. This aligns with the measurement mode (wearable_sensor) and the mechanism (physiological arousal via heart rate). Other leaves focus on HRV, ECG, or broader HR monitoring, but 'heart_rate_monitor' is the closest, most specific operational construct for HR spikes in this EMA/daily diary context.
predictor_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Autonomic arousal: HR-spike counts (wearable) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Objective autonomic markers (heart rate spikes) index physiological arousal during episodes and may precede subjective intensity. | Intervention examples: Used for monitoring and biofeedback; increases self-awareness and guides timing of downregulation exercises. | Measurement: wearable_sensor | Count of HR spikes above baseline threshold in each 'since-last-prompt' window (and daily total); also % time above threshold | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Personalization_Monitoring_and_Data_Feedback / Wearables_and_Home_Monitoring

predictor_variables.label: Psychoeducation engagement about somatic sensations
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Targeted psychoeducation (normalizing sensations, arousal-safety knowledge) reduces threat interpretations and improves acceptance of reassurance.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Short audio/text modules explaining benign causes of common sensations, why tests can be reassuring even if sensations persist.

measurement.channel: app engagement logs (module completion) + daily self-report
measurement.item_or_signal: Number of psychoeducation modules completed yesterday (passive) and self-report rating of how helpful the material was (1-9) in morning diary
measurement.notes: Engagement metric can indicate dose–response relation with reassurance acceptance and threat appraisal.

expected_effects.rationale: Psychoeducation helps reinterpret test results and increases acceptance over days.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure content is validating and not invalidating; do not give medical advice beyond psychoeducation.

feasibility.notes: Engagement logs are reliable when modules delivered via app.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching leaf is psychoeducation_body_sensations_and_appraisal. The variable describes 'Psychoeducation engagement about somatic sensations' and measurement of modules completed (yesterday) and helpfulness, which aligns with psychoeducation modules about body sensations and their appraisal. This is the most specific psychoeducation leaf relevant to somatic/interoceptive processing among candidates, supporting threat appraisal reduction and reassurance acceptance as mechanism.
predictor_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Psychoeducation engagement about somatic sensations | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Targeted psychoeducation (normalizing sensations, arousal-safety knowledge) reduces threat interpretations and improves acceptance of reassurance. | Intervention examples: Short audio/text modules explaining benign causes of common sensations, why tests can be reassuring even if sensations persist. | Measurement: behavioral_log | Number of psychoeducation modules completed yesterday (passive) and self-report rating of how helpful the material was (1-9) in morning diary | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Foundations_and_Common_Factors / Psychoeducation_Modules

edges.notes: Reappraisal is expected to reduce immediate threat appraisals within hours.
Interoceptive exposure should reduce interoceptive hypervigilance over hours to days.
Graded exposure increases leaving-home behavior and reduces avoidance over daily lags.
HR spike counts likely co-occur with or precede self-reported somatic episode intensity in the same window.
Better sleep expected to reduce next-day somatic sensitivity and intensity.
Distress-tolerance breathing can rapidly reduce distress about unpredictable symptoms.
Psychoeducation can increase acceptance of reassurance over days.
Interoceptive exposure is expected to reduce perceived intensity via inhibitory learning.
Exposure/activation should decrease functional interference over several days as confidence increases.

expected_collinearity_risks: P01 (reappraisal) vs P08 (psychoeducation) — both target threat appraisals; monitor for collinearity and consider combining or orthogonalizing if highly correlated.
C01 (somatic intensity) vs P07 (HR spikes) — objective and subjective measurements correlated; avoid treating both as identical constructs in final model.
C02 (unpredictability distress) vs C03 (threat appraisal) — conceptual overlap; ensure items are distinct and use factor checks.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: C04 (reassurance acceptance) may show floor/ceiling effects if user rarely receives medical reassurance; consider weekly supplementary item if ceiling observed.
P07 (HR spikes) may be zero-inflated in participants without wearables or low arousal.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Use 1–9 Likert scaling for EMA items to increase within-person variance.
Capture both subjective (EMA) and objective (GPS, steps, HR spikes) signals for behavioral/physiological constructs to improve signal/noise.
Allow optional event flags (e.g., episode occurred Y/N) plus intensity rating to handle rare but intense events.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Neuromodulation (tES/TMS) and pharmacotherapy are deprioritized at initial observational stage due to complexity, safety, and lower feasibility for ecological momentary sampling.
Multiple overlapping cognitive constructs (e.g., both attention-bias training and broad cognitive restructuring) — selected reappraisal as parsimonious cognitive lever.
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: Model with 7 criteria and 8 predictors (15 nodes) with dense repeated-measures (5x daily EMA + daily diaries + passive streams) provides adequate within-person observations (>=100 per variable expected) for 1st-stage gVAR estimation after person-mean centering and detrending. Monitor missingness and apply regularization/penalized VAR estimation if needed.

criteria.label: Acute somatic symptom intensity and frequency
criteria.query_text_used: Acute somatic symptom intensity and frequency | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how intense were any stomach cramps, nausea, heart racing, or dizziness at their worst? (Rate the worst intensity you felt) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Use single worst-intensity rating per window; optionally add an event flag if an episode occurred (yes/no) for secondary analyses.

mapping.rationale: Variable label is Acute somatic symptom intensity and frequency with EMA self-report assessing multiple interoceptive signals (stomach cramps, nausea, heart racing, dizziness) for the worst intensity since last prompt. There is no exact leaf for multi-symptom acute interoceptive intensity. The closest operational match among candidates is somatic_symptom_burden_high, which captures elevated somatic symptom experience across domains. This leaf provides a single, parsable operational construct aligned with the intended domain (somatic symptom burden/intensity) suitable for initial gVAR-ready mapping while avoiding overfitting to a single symptom-type.

source_variable.label: Acute somatic symptom intensity and frequency

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how intense were any stomach cramps, nausea, heart racing, or dizziness at their worst? (Rate the worst intensity you felt)
measurement.notes: Use single worst-intensity rating per window; optionally add an event flag if an episode occurred (yes/no) for secondary analyses.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; event counts log1p if used

criteria.label: Distress from symptom unpredictability
criteria.query_text_used: Distress from symptom unpredictability | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did unpredictable symptoms (coming without warning) make you feel distressed or upset? | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures momentary intolerance/distress about unpredictability; sensitive to skill use (predictors).

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Distress from symptom unpredictability' with EMA self-report (since_last_prompt) asks about distress in response to unpredictable symptoms. This directly maps to the distress_in_response_to_cues leaf (operational cue-driven distress) as the most specific fit among candidates, capturing momentary distress triggered by unpredictable interoceptive cues within the EMA framework.

criteria.label: Threat interpretation of bodily sensations
criteria.query_text_used: Threat interpretation of bodily sensations | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you interpret bodily sensations as indicating something dangerous (e.g., 'this means something is wrong')? | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Target for cognitive reappraisal and psychoeducation predictors; expected to respond within hours to cognitive skill use.

mapping.rationale: Variable label maps to threat interpretation of bodily sensations during EMA; best-matching leaf is catastrophizing_bodily_signs (idx 29142) in somatic symptom disorder domain, representing excessive threat attribution to bodily cues. Measurement item asks extent sensations are interpreted as dangerous; aligns with cognitive reappraisal/psychoeducation predictors.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you interpret bodily sensations as indicating something dangerous (e.g., 'this means something is wrong')?
measurement.notes: Target for cognitive reappraisal and psychoeducation predictors; expected to respond within hours to cognitive skill use.

criteria.label: Low acceptance of medical reassurance / perceived invalidation
criteria.query_text_used: Low acceptance of medical reassurance / perceived invalidation | Operational item/signal: Thinking about yesterday and any medical feedback you've had, how much did tests or clinician reassurance reduce your concern? (1 = not at all/reassurance increased mistrust, 9 = reassurance greatly reduced concern) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Because reassurance events are relatively rare, the item measures perceived impact; considered lower-frequency but important for functional trust modelling.

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Low acceptance of medical reassurance / perceived invalidation' maps to health reassurance dynamics. The end-of-day EMA item asks how much tests/clinician reassurance reduced yesterday's concern. The most specific leaf matching this mechanism is recurrent_healthcare_reassurance_seeking (Somatic symptom and related disorders), capturing repeated reassurance-seeking behavior in medical contexts. This aligns with threat appraisal, interoceptive vigilance, and response to reassurance motifs in the pseudoprofile, and uses EMA self-report signals.

source_variable.label: Low acceptance of medical reassurance / perceived invalidation

measurement.item_or_signal: Thinking about yesterday and any medical feedback you've had, how much did tests or clinician reassurance reduce your concern? (1 = not at all/reassurance increased mistrust, 9 = reassurance greatly reduced concern)
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: 1-9 Likert (1=made it worse/none, 9=greatly reduced concern) — note: for model consistent polarity we will invert so higher = worse (low acceptance)
measurement.notes: Because reassurance events are relatively rare, the item measures perceived impact; considered lower-frequency but important for functional trust modelling.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: invert then within-person z-score; treat as lower-frequency variable

criteria.label: Avoidance of leaving home / travel avoidance
criteria.query_text_used: Avoidance of leaving home / travel avoidance | Operational item/signal: Overall yesterday, to what extent did you avoid going far from home because of fear of symptoms? (1 = did not avoid, 9 = avoided completely) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Supported by passive GPS-derived max_distance_from_home (daily) to provide objective validation and to separate perceived avoidance vs actual movement.

mapping.rationale: Best match among leaves: agoraphobia-related avoidance of outdoor exposure; label 'fear of being outside alone / avoidance_outdoors_when_alone' directly maps to 'Avoidance of leaving home / travel avoidance'. Measurement item is end-of-day EMA self-report about how much yesterday involved avoiding going far from home; channel supports via GPS to validate avoidance. The other leaves are more specific (public transport, enclosed spaces, etc.) or less directly aligned with leaving home.

source_variable.label: Avoidance of leaving home / travel avoidance

measurement.channel: smartphone daily diary (supported by passive GPS)
measurement.item_or_signal: Overall yesterday, to what extent did you avoid going far from home because of fear of symptoms? (1 = did not avoid, 9 = avoided completely)
measurement.notes: Supported by passive GPS-derived max_distance_from_home (daily) to provide objective validation and to separate perceived avoidance vs actual movement.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; GPS distance log1p for skew

criteria.label: Interoceptive hypervigilance (attention to bodily sensations)
criteria.query_text_used: Interoceptive hypervigilance (attention to bodily sensations) | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent were you paying close attention to your bodily sensations (checking, scanning, monitoring)? | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Direct target of interoceptive exposure and psychoeducation; likely to fluctuate with practice and arousal.

mapping.rationale: Variable label indicates general interoceptive hypervigilance to bodily sensations (attention/monitoring of internal cues). The closest and most specific CRITERION leaf is idx 27107: hypervigilance_to_body_sensations, which captures vigilance to bodily cues across bodily domains rather than modality-specific subtypes (thoracic, GI, etc.) or misinterpretation. The measurement item asks about paying close attention to bodily sensations since the last prompt, which directly operationalizes interoceptive attention/hypervigilance. This aligns with the pseudoprofile’s interoceptive hypervigilance criterion and supports EMA-based, momentary assessment of this mechanism.

criteria.label: Functional and emotional impact (annoyance, fear-driven interference)
criteria.query_text_used: Functional and emotional impact (annoyance, fear-driven interference) | Operational item/signal: Overall yesterday, how much did symptoms or related behaviors (avoidance, worry) interfere with your daily activities or cause distress? | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Daily capture of overall impact reduces redundancy with high-frequency momentary items.

mapping.rationale: Direct match: Functional impairment due to symptoms is exactly what the variable measures (interference with daily activities and distress from symptoms/avoidance/worry); the item is end-of-day EMA via smartphone diary over a 24-hour recall, aligning with the target criterion's focus on functional/emotional impact and avoidance/worry aspects in the pseudoprofile.

source_variable.label: Functional and emotional impact (annoyance, fear-driven interference)

measurement.item_or_signal: Overall yesterday, how much did symptoms or related behaviors (avoidance, worry) interfere with your daily activities or cause distress?
measurement.notes: Daily capture of overall impact reduces redundancy with high-frequency momentary items.

predictors.label: Cognitive reappraisal / threat-interpretation reframing (skill use)
predictors.query_text_used: Cognitive reappraisal / threat-interpretation reframing (skill use) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Cognitive reappraisal reduces catastrophic threat appraisals and downstream anxiety, decreasing avoidance and functional impact. | Intervention examples: Practice labeling thoughts and reframing 'danger' interpretations to benign explanations; short guided reappraisal exercises via app. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, to what extent did you use a cognitive reappraisal or reframe (e.g., reinterpreting sensations as non-dangerous)? | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies / Cognitive_Restructuring

mapping.rationale: Best match is the leaf 'threat_appraisal_reframe' within Cognitive Reappraisal. The variable describes using cognitive reappraisal to reinterpret threat/sensations as non-dangerous, which directly maps to reframing threat appraisals. The measurement is EMA self-report of 'to what extent did you use a cognitive reappraisal or reframe', aligning with the threat_appraisal_reframe construct. This is more specific than general cognitive restructuring and directly targets threat interpretation, fitting the intended predictor as a momentary psycho-behavioral lever.
mapping.leaf_full_path: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / cognitive_restructuring / emotion_linked_reappraisal / threat_and_anxiety / threat_appraisal_reframe

source_variable.label: Cognitive reappraisal / threat-interpretation reframing (skill use)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Cognitive reappraisal reduces catastrophic threat appraisals and downstream anxiety, decreasing avoidance and functional impact.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Practice labeling thoughts and reframing 'danger' interpretations to benign explanations; short guided reappraisal exercises via app.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you use a cognitive reappraisal or reframe (e.g., reinterpreting sensations as non-dangerous)?
measurement.notes: Records both use and intensity of cognitive skill; useful for temporal coupling with threat appraisals.

expected_effects.rationale: Reappraisal is expected to reduce threat interpretations within hours.
Reducing threat appraisals reduces distress about unpredictability over short-to-medium lags.
Sustained reappraisal can reduce functional impact over longer windows.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Safe; ensure guidance avoids invalidating user experience while encouraging reinterpretation.

feasibility.notes: Simple self-report item; expected stable compliance with momentary EMA.

predictors.label: Interoceptive exposure / somatic experiential practice
predictors.query_text_used: Interoceptive exposure / somatic experiential practice | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Planned, guided interoceptive exposures (e.g., intentionally eliciting sensations in safe context) aim to recalibrate sensitivity and reduce catastrophic appraisals and hypervigilance. | Intervention examples: Short guided exercises (e.g., light hyperventilation, spinning chair when safe) under instruction or simulated exposures like holding breath exercises — done only with appropriate guidance and contraindications screened. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, did you do any interoceptive exposure or body-focused practice? If yes, rate how much (1=not at all, 9=intense session). | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies / Exposure_and_Inhibitory_Learning

mapping.rationale: Best match: Interoceptive exposure implemented as part of exposure/inhibitory-learning protocol within CBT. The variable 'Interoceptive exposure / somatic experiential practice' corresponds to actual interoceptive exposure activities (e.g., breathing/heartbeat sensations) measured via EMA as 'did you do any interoceptive exposure' with intensity rating. Leaf 2607 precisely encodes 'interoceptive_exposure_when_indicated' under Exposure_and_Inhibitory_Learning, capturing the targeted mechanism of recalibrating sensitivity and reducing catastrophic appraisals and hypervigilance. Other leaves either describe framework, safety behaviors, or more distant concepts; this one aligns with the measurement item and the intended predictor mechanism.

source_variable.label: Interoceptive exposure / somatic experiential practice
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Planned, guided interoceptive exposures (e.g., intentionally eliciting sensations in safe context) aim to recalibrate sensitivity and reduce catastrophic appraisals and hypervigilance.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Short guided exercises (e.g., light hyperventilation, spinning chair when safe) under instruction or simulated exposures like holding breath exercises — done only with appropriate guidance and contraindications screened.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you do any interoceptive exposure or body-focused practice? If yes, rate how much (1=not at all, 9=intense session).
measurement.notes: Also capture type/count in optional free-text for exploratory analyses. Safety: avoid unsupervised high-risk exposures; include screening before recommending.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; exposure counts log1p if used

expected_effects.rationale: Interoceptive exposure should reduce hypervigilance and scanning over hours to days.
Exposure may reduce reported symptom intensity or the perceived peak via learning processes.
Relearning about sensations reduces threat appraisals over days.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Interoceptive exposures should be introduced with clinician oversight when possible; avoid exposures contraindicated by medical conditions.

feasibility.notes: Self-report of practice feasible; adherence may vary.

predictors.label: Distress-tolerance breathing / downregulation practice
predictors.query_text_used: Distress-tolerance breathing / downregulation practice | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Short breathing or grounding practices rapidly downregulate sympathetic arousal and can reduce episode severity and distress. | Intervention examples: Guided diaphragmatic breathing, paced breathing apps, 4-4-6 breathing, grounding via 5 senses. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, did you use a breathing or grounding technique to manage symptoms? Rate use intensity (1=not at all, 9=very much). | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Third_Wave_and_Contextual_Therapies / DBT_Skills_Based_Therapy

mapping.rationale: Variable label corresponds to distress-tolerance breathing within DBT Skills-Based Therapy (Third Wave/Contextual). The measurement item (EMA self-report: 'Since the last prompt, did you use a breathing or grounding technique...? Rate use intensity 1-9') directly maps to the leaf 'breathing_to_downshift_arousal' under distress_tolerance_skills. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates for a distress-tolerance breathing/downregulation practice in the DBT framework, aligning with the pseudoprofile emphasis on distress-tolerance and rapid arousal downregulation. The context supports momentary EMA capture of practice usage and intensity.

source_variable.label: Distress-tolerance breathing / downregulation practice
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Short breathing or grounding practices rapidly downregulate sympathetic arousal and can reduce episode severity and distress.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Guided diaphragmatic breathing, paced breathing apps, 4-4-6 breathing, grounding via 5 senses.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you use a breathing or grounding technique to manage symptoms? Rate use intensity (1=not at all, 9=very much).
measurement.notes: Short, low-burden item that may show quick effects within the same prompt window.

expected_effects.rationale: Breathing can acutely reduce intensity and perceived severity in the short term.
Rapid downregulation reduces immediate distress about unpredictability.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Safe; encourage medical clearance if major cardiopulmonary disease.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility and expected within-person variance.

predictors.label: Graded behavioral exposure (leaving-home attempts / activation)
predictors.query_text_used: Graded behavioral exposure (leaving-home attempts / activation) | Domain: PSYCHOxSOCIAL | Mechanism: Planned, graded attempts to leave home counter avoidance, promote inhibitory learning, and increase confidence functioning outside home. | Intervention examples: Short stepwise outings (e.g., to end of street, short walk to nearby store) with pre-specified goals and coping plans. | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Passive: max_distance_from_home (meters) in last 24h and per-prompt window. Self-report (daily): rate degree of leaving-home attempt/effort (1-9). | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies / Behavioral_Experiments_and_Learning

mapping.rationale: Best-match leaf: graduated_exposure_principles under PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / exposure_and_inhibitory_learning / design_and_rationale / hierarchy_and_targeting. It directly encodes graded exposure principles for leaving-home activation, aligning with the var label Graded behavioral exposure and its mechanism (graded attempts to leave home, inhibitory learning, confidence). The measurement plan (GPS distance_from_home + daily self-report of leaving-home attempts) fits exposure/activation monitoring within a gVAR context.

source_variable.label: Graded behavioral exposure (leaving-home attempts / activation)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Planned, graded attempts to leave home counter avoidance, promote inhibitory learning, and increase confidence functioning outside home.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Short stepwise outings (e.g., to end of street, short walk to nearby store) with pre-specified goals and coping plans.

measurement.channel: smartphone GPS + smartphone daily diary for intention/effort
measurement.item_or_signal: Passive: max_distance_from_home (meters) in last 24h and per-prompt window. Self-report (daily): rate degree of leaving-home attempt/effort (1-9).
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: GPS distance in meters (daily); 1-9 Likert for self-report
measurement.recall_window: past 24 hours (daily) and per-prompt windows for GPS when available
measurement.notes: GPS-derived objective behavior reduces self-report bias. Privacy: compute distance_from_home on-device or send aggregated metrics only.

expected_effects.rationale: Successful graded exposure should reduce avoidance (daily).
Behavioral activation reduces functional impact over days.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure exposures are safe and feasible; include emergency planning for severe episodes.

feasibility.notes: Passive GPS may be unavailable for some users; missingness must be handled.

predictors.label: Sleep routine / last-night sleep quality
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep routine / last-night sleep quality | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Poor sleep increases physiological reactivity and lowers tolerance for somatic sensations; improving sleep may reduce somatic episode frequency/intensity and daily impact. | Intervention examples: Regular bed/wake times, stimulus control, brief sleep hygiene adjustments. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | How well did you sleep last night? (1 = very poorly, 9 = very well). Also capture total sleep time estimate (hours). | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration / Sleep_Routines_and_Practices

mapping.rationale: The variable Sleep routine / last-night sleep quality maps to BIO Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration / Sleep_Routines_and_Practices. Among the candidate leaves, Sleep_diary (idx 4549) is the best match because it represents a daily self-assessment log (morning diary) that captures both sleep quality and total sleep time. This aligns with measurement.mode EMA_self_report and measurement.item_or_signal (last night sleep quality and hours slept). It is the most specific operational construct for a daily sleep routine measure within the Sleep_Routines_and_Practices domain, whereas Sleep_quality_rating alone would miss duration data. The pseudoprofile notes emphasize daily-lag analyses using morning diaries, which 4549 directly supports.

source_variable.label: Sleep routine / last-night sleep quality
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Poor sleep increases physiological reactivity and lowers tolerance for somatic sensations; improving sleep may reduce somatic episode frequency/intensity and daily impact.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Regular bed/wake times, stimulus control, brief sleep hygiene adjustments.

measurement.item_or_signal: How well did you sleep last night? (1 = very poorly, 9 = very well). Also capture total sleep time estimate (hours).
measurement.notes: Morning diary captures sleep quality and duration for daily-lag analyses with symptoms.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score for Likert; log1p if duration skewed

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep may reduce next-day somatic sensitivity and episode intensity.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Safe; flag for referral if severe insomnia or sleep disorders suspected.

feasibility.notes: Morning diary is low-burden and reliable for sleep quality.

predictors.label: Physical activity (daily steps)
predictors.query_text_used: Physical activity (daily steps) | Domain: BIOxPSYCHO | Mechanism: Physical activity may reduce avoidance, improve mood and resilience, and reduce functional impact over days. | Intervention examples: Daily walking goals, short structured walks as exposure/activation. | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Total steps in last 24 hours (phone or wearable-derived) | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Movement_Physical_Function_and_Somatic_Care / Aerobic_and_Cardiorespiratory_Movement

mapping.rationale: Variable maps to Physical activity (daily steps) captured via wearable/phone sensor. The most specific PREDICTOR leaf is step_counting_wearable (idx 4580) under wearables_home_monitoring/physiology/activity_fitness_and_biomechanics/activity_and_energy. This leaf directly corresponds to the measurement item 'Total steps in last 24 hours' derived from a wearable or phone pedometer, with measurement mode 'passive_daily_aggregate' and unit 'steps/day'. This aligns with the variable label and intended use as an objective, passive activation/activation-support signal reflecting leaving-home/activity, and is more specific than broader activity_tracker or lifestyle_walk-related leaves.
mapping.leaf_full_path: BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / activity_fitness_and_biomechanics / activity_and_energy / step_counting_wearable

source_variable.label: Physical activity (daily steps)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Physical activity may reduce avoidance, improve mood and resilience, and reduce functional impact over days.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Daily walking goals, short structured walks as exposure/activation.

measurement.item_or_signal: Total steps in last 24 hours (phone or wearable-derived)
measurement.notes: Objective metric for activation and a correlate of leaving-home behavior.

expected_effects.rationale: More steps correlate with leaving-home and reduced avoidance.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Consider privacy; share only aggregated counts.

feasibility.notes: Phone step counts available for many but not all users; missingness expected.

predictors.label: Autonomic arousal: HR-spike counts (wearable)
predictors.query_text_used: Autonomic arousal: HR-spike counts (wearable) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Objective autonomic markers (heart rate spikes) index physiological arousal during episodes and may precede subjective intensity. | Intervention examples: Used for monitoring and biofeedback; increases self-awareness and guides timing of downregulation exercises. | Measurement: wearable_sensor | Count of HR spikes above baseline threshold in each 'since-last-prompt' window (and daily total); also % time above threshold | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Personalization_Monitoring_and_Data_Feedback / Wearables_and_Home_Monitoring

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Autonomic arousal: HR-spike counts (wearable)' corresponds to objective heart rate data captured via wearable devices. The most specific leaf among candidates is 'heart_rate_monitor' under wearables_and_home_monitoring, which directly represents HR measurement from wearable sensors. This aligns with the measurement mode (wearable_sensor) and the mechanism (physiological arousal via heart rate). Other leaves focus on HRV, ECG, or broader HR monitoring, but 'heart_rate_monitor' is the closest, most specific operational construct for HR spikes in this EMA/daily diary context.

source_variable.label: Autonomic arousal: HR-spike counts (wearable)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Objective autonomic markers (heart rate spikes) index physiological arousal during episodes and may precede subjective intensity.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Used for monitoring and biofeedback; increases self-awareness and guides timing of downregulation exercises.

measurement.channel: consumer wearable (e.g., wrist-worn HR sensor) or phone-based HR if available
measurement.item_or_signal: Count of HR spikes above baseline threshold in each 'since-last-prompt' window (and daily total); also % time above threshold
measurement.notes: Important objective correlate of C01; missing for those without wearables — treat as optional stratified analysis.

expected_effects.rationale: Higher HR spike counts are associated with reports of stronger/visible somatic episodes in the same window.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Wearable data sensitive; obtain consent and process aggregated metrics only.

feasibility.notes: Useful when available but will be missing for many; include as auxiliary variable rather than required.

predictors.label: Psychoeducation engagement about somatic sensations
predictors.query_text_used: Psychoeducation engagement about somatic sensations | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Targeted psychoeducation (normalizing sensations, arousal-safety knowledge) reduces threat interpretations and improves acceptance of reassurance. | Intervention examples: Short audio/text modules explaining benign causes of common sensations, why tests can be reassuring even if sensations persist. | Measurement: behavioral_log | Number of psychoeducation modules completed yesterday (passive) and self-report rating of how helpful the material was (1-9) in morning diary | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Foundations_and_Common_Factors / Psychoeducation_Modules

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is psychoeducation_body_sensations_and_appraisal. The variable describes 'Psychoeducation engagement about somatic sensations' and measurement of modules completed (yesterday) and helpfulness, which aligns with psychoeducation modules about body sensations and their appraisal. This is the most specific psychoeducation leaf relevant to somatic/interoceptive processing among candidates, supporting threat appraisal reduction and reassurance acceptance as mechanism.

source_variable.label: Psychoeducation engagement about somatic sensations
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Targeted psychoeducation (normalizing sensations, arousal-safety knowledge) reduces threat interpretations and improves acceptance of reassurance.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Short audio/text modules explaining benign causes of common sensations, why tests can be reassuring even if sensations persist.

measurement.channel: app engagement logs (module completion) + daily self-report
measurement.item_or_signal: Number of psychoeducation modules completed yesterday (passive) and self-report rating of how helpful the material was (1-9) in morning diary
measurement.notes: Engagement metric can indicate dose–response relation with reassurance acceptance and threat appraisal.

expected_effects.rationale: Psychoeducation helps reinterpret test results and increases acceptance over days.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure content is validating and not invalidating; do not give medical advice beyond psychoeducation.

feasibility.notes: Engagement logs are reliable when modules delivered via app.
